annotations.label: Circle (lead)

anchor.snippet: A circle is a shape consisting of all points in a plane that are at a given distance from a given point

annotations.note: Mathlib defines `Metric.sphere x ε := { y | dist y x = ε }` in any metric space; a planar circle is the special case in a 2D Euclidean space.

annotations.label: Radius (lead)

anchor.snippet: The distance between any point of the circle and the centre is called the radius

annotations.note: The bundled `EuclideanGeometry.Sphere` carries a `radius : ℝ` field with the corresponding `mem_sphere` characterization.

annotations.label: Diameter (lead)

anchor.snippet: The length of a line segment connecting two points on the circle and passing through the centre is called the diameter

annotations.note: `Sphere.IsDiameter` says two points are endpoints of a diameter (midpoint is the center); `IsDiameter.dist_left_right` shows the length equals `2 * radius`.

annotations.label: Disc (lead)

anchor.snippet: A circle bounds a region of the plane called a disc

annotations.note: `Metric.closedBall x ε := { y | dist y x ≤ ε }` is the disc when specialized to `EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2)`; `Complex.UnitDisc` is the unit-disc instance.

annotations.label: Annulus

anchor.snippet: Annulus : a ring-shaped object, the region bounded by two concentric circles

annotations.note: No dedicated annulus definition exists in Mathlib; it would have to be expressed as a set difference of two `Metric.closedBall`s.

annotations.label: Arc

anchor.snippet: Arc : any connected part of a circle

annotations.note: Mathlib has no definition of a circular arc as a connected subset of a circle.

annotations.label: Centre

anchor.snippet: Centre : the point equidistant from all points on the circle

annotations.note: The bundled `EuclideanGeometry.Sphere` structure has a `center : P` field.

annotations.label: Chord

anchor.snippet: Chord : a line segment whose endpoints lie on the circle

annotations.note: There is no `Chord` definition, but chords are routinely handled as pairs of points on a sphere, e.g. in the perpendicular-bisector lemma above.

annotations.label: Circumference

anchor.snippet: Circumference : the length of one circuit along the circle

annotations.note: Mathlib has no `circumference` definition; arc/path length of a parametrised circle is not specialized to a named lemma.

annotations.label: Diameter

anchor.snippet: Diameter : a line segment whose endpoints lie on the circle and that passes through the centre

annotations.note: `Sphere.IsDiameter` (and the constructor `Sphere.ofDiameter`) bundles the notion of a diameter chord.

annotations.label: Disc

anchor.snippet: Disc : the region of the plane bounded by a circle

annotations.note: The closed disc is the special case of `Metric.closedBall` in 2D Euclidean space.

annotations.label: Lens

anchor.snippet: Lens : the region common to (the intersection of) two overlapping discs

annotations.note: No `lens` definition; would just be the intersection of two `Metric.closedBall`s.

annotations.label: Radius

anchor.snippet: Radius : a line segment joining the centre of a circle with any single point on the circle itself

annotations.note: Captured as the `radius` field of a `Sphere` together with `mem_sphere`.

annotations.label: Sector

anchor.snippet: Sector : a region bounded by two radii of equal length with a common centre

annotations.note: Mathlib has no `sector` definition.

annotations.label: Segment

anchor.snippet: Segment : a region bounded by a chord and one of the arcs connecting the chord's endpoints

annotations.note: No circular-segment definition exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Secant

anchor.snippet: Secant : an extended chord, a coplanar straight line, intersecting a circle in two points

annotations.note: Mathlib has no separate `secant` definition; secant configurations appear only via lemmas about lines through two points on a sphere.

annotations.label: Semicircle

anchor.snippet: Semicircle : one of the two possible arcs determined by the endpoints of a diameter

annotations.note: No dedicated semicircle definition; the diameter structure exists via `Sphere.IsDiameter` but the two arcs themselves are not defined.

annotations.label: Tangent

anchor.snippet: Tangent : a coplanar straight line that has one single point in common with a circle

annotations.note: `Sphere.IsTangentAt` defines an affine subspace being tangent to a sphere at a point; this covers tangent lines to circles.

annotations.label: Euclid's definition of a circle

anchor.snippet: A circle is a plane figure bounded by one curved line

annotations.note: Captured equivalently by `Metric.sphere` (locus at fixed distance), which is Euclid's definition once specialised to the plane.

annotations.label: Lindemann: π is transcendental

anchor.snippet: Ferdinand von Lindemann proved that π is transcendental

annotations.note: Mathlib has the analytic-part of Lindemann–Weierstrass but no final `Transcendental ℤ Real.pi` theorem; grep finds no such statement.

annotations.label: Circumference-to-diameter ratio is π

anchor.snippet: The ratio of a circle's circumference to its diameter is π

annotations.note: Mathlib has `Real.pi` but no theorem identifying circumference (path length of a circle) with `π · diameter`.

annotations.label: Archimedes: area of a circle

anchor.snippet: the area enclosed by a circle is equal to that of a triangle whose base has the length of the circle's circumference and whose height equals the circle's radius

annotations.note: `Theorems100.area_disc` proves `volume (disc r) = π · r^2`, the equivalent statement of Archimedes' theorem.

annotations.label: Isoperimetric property of the circle

anchor.snippet: The circle is the plane curve enclosing the maximum area for a given arc length

annotations.note: Grep for `isoperimetric` returns no Mathlib results.

annotations.label: Radian measure

anchor.snippet: the radian measure 𝜃 of the angle is the ratio of the arc length to the radius

annotations.note: Angles are represented in radians via `Real.Angle`, but the defining ratio (arc length ÷ radius) is not stated as a theorem.

annotations.label: One radian

anchor.snippet: One radian is the measure of the central angle subtended by a circular arc whose length is equal to its radius

annotations.note: The radian is implicit in `Real.Angle`/`Real.pi`, but there is no theorem `1 rad ↔ arc = radius`.

annotations.label: Arc length formula

anchor.snippet: the formula for the arc length s of a circular arc of radius r and subtending a central angle of measure

annotations.note: No `arc_length = r · θ` lemma exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Sector area formula

anchor.snippet: the formula for the area A of a circular sector of radius r and with central angle of measure

annotations.note: No sector area lemma; sectors are not defined.

annotations.label: Cartesian equation of a circle

anchor.snippet: the circle with centre coordinates ( a , b ) and radius r is the set of all points ( x , y ) such that

annotations.note: `Metric.sphere` in `EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2)` gives the locus `dist (x,y) (a,b) = r`, but Mathlib never states it in the textbook coordinate form `(x-a)^2 + (y-b)^2 = r^2`.

annotations.label: Equation of a circle centred at the origin

anchor.snippet: If the circle is centred at the origin (0, 0), then the equation simplifies to

annotations.note: Special case of `Metric.sphere 0 r`; the explicit `x^2 + y^2 = r^2` characterisation in coordinates is not stated.

annotations.label: Parametric form of a circle

anchor.snippet: The equation can be written in parametric form using the trigonometric functions sine and cosine

annotations.note: `circleMap c R θ = c + R · exp(θ · I)` is the standard cosine/sine parametrisation expressed in `ℂ`.

annotations.label: Alternative (stereographic) parametrisation

anchor.snippet: An alternative parametrisation of the circle is

annotations.note: Mathlib has `stereographic` (and `stereographic'`) projection charts for spheres in inner product spaces; the explicit rational parametrisation of a circle is not separately stated.

annotations.label: Three-point equation of a circle

anchor.snippet: The equation of the circle determined by three points

annotations.note: The determinant form for the circle through three points is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Circular points at infinity characterise circles

anchor.snippet: It can be proven that a conic section is a circle exactly when it contains

annotations.note: Projective characterization of circles via circular points at infinity is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Polar equation of a circle

anchor.snippet: In polar coordinates , the equation of a circle is

annotations.note: No polar-form equation of a circle is stated; `PolarCoord` exists but no specialisation to circles.

annotations.label: Complex equation of a circle

anchor.snippet: a circle with a centre at c and radius r has the equation

annotations.note: In `ℂ`, `Metric.sphere c r = {z | dist z c = r}` is the same locus, but Mathlib does not state it as `|z - c| = r` in the Wikipedia form.

annotations.label: Generalised circle (complex plane)

anchor.snippet: is sometimes called a generalised circle

annotations.note: No `generalised circle` (line-or-circle on the Riemann sphere) definition in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Tangent perpendicular to diameter

anchor.snippet: The tangent line through a point P on the circle is perpendicular to the diameter passing through P

annotations.note: `IsTangentAt p line[ℝ, p, q] ↔ ∠ q p s.center = π/2`, i.e. tangency is equivalent to the line being perpendicular to the radius (and hence the diameter) at the contact point.

annotations.label: Equation of the tangent line

anchor.snippet: Evaluating at ( x 1 , y 1 ) determines the value of c , and the result is that the equation of the tangent is

annotations.note: The explicit Cartesian equation `(x₁-a)(x-a)+(y₁-b)(y-b)=r²` for the tangent line is not formalised.

annotations.label: Isoperimetric property (Properties)

anchor.snippet: The circle is the shape with the largest area for a given length of perimeter

annotations.note: The isoperimetric inequality for plane curves is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Symmetry group of the circle

anchor.snippet: Its symmetry group is the orthogonal group O(2, R )

annotations.note: The orthogonal group `O(n, R)` is defined as `Matrix.orthogonalGroup`, but it is not stated as the symmetry group of the circle.

annotations.label: All circles are similar

anchor.snippet: All circles are similar

annotations.note: No theorem asserting similarity of all circles.

annotations.label: Circumference proportional to radius

anchor.snippet: A circle circumference and radius are proportional

annotations.note: No `circumference` definition or proportionality lemma is available.

annotations.label: Area proportional to radius squared

anchor.snippet: The area enclosed and the square of its radius are proportional

annotations.note: `volume (disc r) = π · r²` directly gives the area-radius² proportionality.

annotations.label: Unit circle

anchor.snippet: The circle that is centred at the origin with radius 1 is called the unit circle

annotations.note: `Circle` is defined as `Submonoid.unitSphere ℂ`, i.e. `{z : ℂ | dist z 0 = 1}`.

annotations.label: Three noncollinear points determine a unique circle

anchor.snippet: Through any three points, not all on the same line, there lies a unique circle

annotations.note: For an affinely independent family there is a unique circumsphere; specialised to three noncollinear points in 2D it gives the unique circumscribed circle (`Affine.Simplex.circumsphere`).

annotations.label: Chords equidistant iff equal in length

anchor.snippet: Chords are equidistant from the centre of a circle if and only if they are equal in length

annotations.note: No direct lemma equating chord length and distance from the centre was found by grep.

annotations.label: Perpendicular bisector of a chord passes through centre

anchor.snippet: The perpendicular bisector of a chord passes through the centre of a circle

annotations.note: Says `⟪s.center -ᵥ midpoint ℝ p₁ p₂, p₂ -ᵥ p₁⟫ = 0`, i.e. the chord is orthogonal to the line from its midpoint to the centre.

annotations.label: Central angle is twice the inscribed angle

anchor.snippet: If a central angle and an inscribed angle of a circle are subtended by the same chord and on the same side of the chord, then the central angle is twice the inscribed angle

annotations.note: `∡ p₁ s.center p₃ = 2 • ∡ p₁ p₂ p₃` for three points on a sphere is exactly the inscribed angle theorem.

annotations.label: Equal inscribed angles on same side

anchor.snippet: If two angles are inscribed on the same chord and on the same side of the chord, then they are equal

annotations.note: Mathlib's `two_zsmul_oangle_eq` is the mod-π version (angles equal up to π), covering both same-side equality and opposite-side supplementarity.

annotations.label: Supplementary inscribed angles on opposite sides

anchor.snippet: If two angles are inscribed on the same chord and on opposite sides of the chord, then they are supplementary

annotations.note: Same `two_zsmul_oangle_eq` lemma: equality of `(2 : ℤ) • ∡` matches angles being either equal or supplementary.

annotations.label: Thales' theorem

anchor.snippet: An inscribed angle subtended by a diameter is a right angle

annotations.note: Mathlib explicitly labels this `thales_theorem` and proves the iff version.

annotations.label: Diameter is the longest chord

anchor.snippet: The diameter is the longest chord of the circle

annotations.note: No named lemma stating `∀ p q ∈ s, dist p q ≤ 2 * s.radius` was found.

annotations.label: Intersecting chords theorem

anchor.snippet: If the intersection of any two chords divides one chord into lengths a and b and divides the other chord into lengths c and d , then ab = cd

annotations.note: Labelled the Intersecting Chords Theorem (Freek #55).

annotations.label: Perpendicular intersecting chords sum to diameter squared

anchor.snippet: If the intersection of any two perpendicular chords divides one chord into lengths a and b

annotations.note: No formalisation of `a² + b² + c² + d² = (2r)²` for perpendicular chords was found.

annotations.label: Sum of squared lengths of perpendicular chords

anchor.snippet: The sum of the squared lengths of any two chords intersecting at right angles at a given point is the same

annotations.note: No such invariant is stated in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Distance-to-chord product identity

anchor.snippet: The distance from a point on the circle to a given chord times the diameter of the circle equals the product of the distances from the point to the ends of the chord

annotations.note: Not present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Perpendicular to radius at endpoint is tangent

anchor.snippet: A line drawn perpendicular to a radius through the end point of the radius lying on the circle is a tangent to the circle

annotations.note: Direct converse: `∠ q p s.center = π/2` and `p ∈ s` implies `s.IsTangentAt p line[ℝ, p, q]`.

annotations.label: Perpendicular to tangent at contact passes through centre

anchor.snippet: A line drawn perpendicular to a tangent through the point of contact with a circle passes through the centre of the circle

annotations.note: `IsTangentAt.angle_eq_pi_div_two` gives `∠ q p s.center = π/2`, i.e. the radius at the contact point is perpendicular to the tangent.

annotations.label: Two tangents from external point are equal

anchor.snippet: Two tangents can always be drawn to a circle from any point outside the circle, and these tangents are equal in length

annotations.note: `IsTangentAt.dist_eq_of_mem_of_mem` proves equal lengths from a common external point on two tangent lines.

annotations.label: Tangent angles at external point are supplementary

anchor.snippet: If a tangent at A and a tangent at B intersect at the exterior point P

annotations.note: No supplementarity lemma for the two angles `∠APO` / `∠APB` was located.

annotations.label: Tangent–chord angle (Tangent)

anchor.snippet: If AD is tangent to the circle at A and if AQ is a chord of the circle

annotations.note: The tangent–chord angle identity is not proved in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Chord theorem

anchor.snippet: The chord theorem states that if two chords, CD and EB , intersect at A , then AC × AD = AB × AE

annotations.note: Same Intersecting Chords Theorem in `Sphere.Power`.

annotations.label: Two secants corollary

anchor.snippet: If two secants, AE and AD , also cut the circle at B and C respectively

annotations.note: The Intersecting Secants Theorem version with collinear (`angle = 0`) external point.

annotations.label: Tangent–secant theorem

anchor.snippet: If a tangent from an external point A meets the circle at F and a secant from the external point A meets the circle at C and D respectively

annotations.note: Stated as `dist p t ^ 2 = dist p a * dist p b`.

annotations.label: Tangent–chord angle theorem

anchor.snippet: The angle between a chord and the tangent at one of its endpoints is equal to one half the angle subtended at the centre of the circle

annotations.note: Not found in Mathlib (the standard tangent–chord/inscribed-angle limiting case is not formalised).

annotations.label: Chord of right central angle has length r√2

anchor.snippet: If the angle subtended by the chord at the centre is 90

annotations.note: No named lemma identifies this specific chord length.

annotations.label: Secant–secant angle theorem

anchor.snippet: If two secants are inscribed in the circle as shown at right, then the measurement of angle A is equal to one half the difference of the measurements of the enclosed arcs

annotations.note: The half-difference of arcs formula for two secants is not formalised.

annotations.label: Inscribed angle theorem

anchor.snippet: An inscribed angle (examples are the blue and green angles in the figure) is exactly half the corresponding central angle

annotations.note: Same statement as 'central angle is twice the inscribed angle', cited in `docs/undergrad.yaml` as the inscribed angle theorem.

annotations.label: Sagitta (versine)

anchor.snippet: The sagitta (also known as the versine ) is a line segment drawn perpendicular to a chord

annotations.note: Grep finds no `sagitta`/`versine` definition.

annotations.label: Radius from chord and sagitta

anchor.snippet: Given the length y of a chord and the length x of the sagitta, the Pythagorean theorem can be used to calculate the radius of the unique circle that will fit around the two lines

annotations.note: No such formula exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Compass construction from centre and point

anchor.snippet: The simplest and most basic is the construction given the centre of the circle and a point on the circle

annotations.note: Compass-and-straightedge constructions are not formalised in Mathlib (only algebraic constructibility appears, in unrelated contexts).

annotations.label: Construction of circle with given diameter

anchor.snippet: Construct the midpoint M of the diameter

annotations.note: Compass-and-straightedge constructions are not formalised.

annotations.label: Construction through three noncollinear points

anchor.snippet: Construct the perpendicular bisector of the segment PQ

annotations.note: Existence and uniqueness of the circumscribed circle is formalised; the compass-and-straightedge construction itself is not.

annotations.label: Circle of Apollonius

anchor.snippet: Apollonius of Perga showed that a circle may also be defined as the set of points in a plane having a constant ratio (other than 1) of distances to two fixed foci

annotations.note: Grep for `Apollonius` returns no Mathlib results.

annotations.label: Apollonius circle via cross-ratio

anchor.snippet: the circle of Apollonius for these three points is the collection of points P for which the absolute value of the cross-ratio is equal to one

annotations.note: Cross-ratio characterisation of Apollonius circles is not formalised.

annotations.label: Apollonius locus is a line when C is midpoint

anchor.snippet: If C is the midpoint of the segment AB , then the collection of points P satisfying the Apollonius condition

annotations.note: Not formalised in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Generalised circle

anchor.snippet: the locus of points P satisfying the above equation is called a "generalised circle."

annotations.note: No `generalised circle` / line-or-circle notion in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Incircle of a triangle

anchor.section: Inscription in or circumscription about other figures
anchor.snippet: In every triangle a unique circle, called the incircle , can be inscribed such that it is tangent to each of the three sides of the triangle

annotations.note: `Affine.Simplex.insphere` (and `incenter`, `inradius`) generalises the triangle incircle to any simplex; the always-exists insphere covers the triangle case.

annotations.label: Circumcircle of a triangle

anchor.section: Inscription in or circumscription about other figures
anchor.snippet: About every triangle a unique circle, called the circumcircle, can be circumscribed such that it goes through each of the triangle's three vertices

annotations.note: Defined and proved unique via `AffineIndependent.existsUnique_dist_eq`.

annotations.label: Tangential polygon

anchor.section: Inscription in or circumscription about other figures
anchor.snippet: A tangential polygon , such as a tangential quadrilateral , is any convex polygon within which a circle can be inscribed that is tangent to each side of the polygon

annotations.note: No `TangentialPolygon` definition in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Cyclic polygon

anchor.section: Inscription in or circumscription about other figures
anchor.snippet: A cyclic polygon is any convex polygon about which a circle can be circumscribed

annotations.note: `Concyclic` captures a set of points being cospherical and coplanar, which is the general notion underlying cyclic polygons, but no `CyclicPolygon` structure is defined.

annotations.label: Bicentric polygon

anchor.section: Inscription in or circumscription about other figures
anchor.snippet: A polygon that is both cyclic and tangential is called a bicentric polygon

annotations.note: No `BicentricPolygon` notion exists.

annotations.label: Hypocycloid

anchor.section: Inscription in or circumscription about other figures
anchor.snippet: A hypocycloid is a curve that is inscribed in a given circle by tracing a fixed point on a smaller circle that rolls within and tangent to the given circle

annotations.note: Grep for `hypocycloid`/`cycloid` finds nothing in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Circle as limit of regular polygons

anchor.snippet: The series of regular polygons with n sides has the circle as its limit as n approaches infinity

annotations.note: No formalisation of regular polygons converging to a circle.

annotations.label: Cartesian oval

anchor.snippet: A Cartesian oval is a set of points such that a weighted sum of the distances from any of its points to two fixed points (foci) is a constant

annotations.note: Cartesian ovals are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Circle as ellipse with eccentricity zero

anchor.snippet: A circle is an ellipse with an eccentricity of zero

annotations.note: No `Ellipse`/`eccentricity` structure for plane conics in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Supercircle

anchor.snippet: A supercircle has b = a

annotations.note: Superellipses/supercircles are not formalised.

annotations.label: Cassini oval

anchor.snippet: A Cassini oval is a set of points such that the product of the distances from any of its points to two fixed points is a constant

annotations.note: Cassini ovals are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Curve of constant width

anchor.snippet: A curve of constant width is a figure whose width, defined as the perpendicular distance between two distinct parallel lines

annotations.note: Constant-width curves are not formalised.

annotations.label: Locus of constant sum of squared distances is a circle

anchor.snippet: The locus of points such that the sum of the squares of the distances to the given points is constant is a circle

annotations.note: Not formalised in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Generalisation to higher powers over regular polygon vertices

anchor.snippet: The locus of points such that the sum of the

annotations.note: Not formalised.

annotations.label: Squaring the circle

anchor.snippet: Squaring the circle is the problem, proposed by ancient geometers , of constructing a square with the same area as a given circle

annotations.note: Compass-and-straightedge constructibility is not formalised in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Impossibility of squaring the circle

anchor.snippet: In 1882, the task was proven to be impossible, as a consequence of the Lindemann–Weierstrass theorem

annotations.note: Depends on transcendence of π and constructibility, neither of which is fully assembled in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Circles in p-norms

anchor.snippet: Defining a circle as the set of points with a fixed distance from a point, different shapes can be considered circles under different definitions of distance

annotations.note: `Metric.sphere` works in any (pseudo)metric space, so the abstract `p`-norm 'circle' is captured, but specific p-norm shapes are not separately developed.

annotations.label: Taxicab circles are squares at 45°

anchor.snippet: Taxicab circles are squares with sides oriented at a 45° angle to the coordinate axes

annotations.note: The taxicab-circle shape characterisation is not stated.

annotations.label: Unit taxicab disc is von Neumann neighborhood

anchor.snippet: A circle of radius 1 (using this distance) is the von Neumann neighborhood of its centre

annotations.note: Von Neumann neighbourhood is not defined in Mathlib (`vonNeumann` results are unrelated, e.g. set theory / operator algebras).

annotations.label: Chebyshev-distance circle is a square

anchor.snippet: A circle of radius r for the Chebyshev distance

annotations.note: No characterisation of Chebyshev `Metric.sphere` as a square is formalised.

annotations.label: Circle as 1-sphere

anchor.snippet: The circle is the one-dimensional hypersphere (the 1-sphere)

annotations.note: `Circle` is the unit sphere in `ℂ`, equivalently the 1-sphere `S¹`; smooth manifold structure is provided in `Mathlib.Geometry.Manifold.Instances.Sphere`.

annotations.label: Topological circle (up to homeomorphism)

anchor.snippet: In topology , a circle is not limited to the geometric concept, but to all of its homeomorphisms

annotations.note: `Circle` has its topology, and `TopCat.Sphere` exists, but Mathlib does not formalise 'a topological circle = any space homeomorphic to S¹'.

annotations.label: Oriented circle

anchor.snippet: An oriented circle is an ordinary circle with an orientation represented by a binary value attached to it

annotations.note: No `OrientedCircle` structure exists; `Module.Oriented` orientations are at the vector-space level, not bundled with circles.
